title: Auth in Django
description: Use Django's built-in auth system to add login, signup, and per-user data.

steps.title: Django ships with auth built in
steps.instruction:
## You already have an auth system

Most frameworks make you build authentication from scratch. Django is different: it ships with a complete auth system in `django.contrib.auth`. It is already installed in every Django project.

What you get for free:

- A `User` model with username, password (hashed automatically), email, and a few flags like `is_staff` and `is_active`
- `authenticate(username, password)` - checks credentials and returns a `User` object or `None`
- `login(request, user)` - attaches the user to the session
- `logout(request)` - clears the session
- `@login_required` - a decorator that redirects anonymous users
- `UserCreationForm` - a ready-made form for signup with password confirmation
- `request.user` - the currently logged-in user, available in every view and template

You do not need to install anything. It is already in `INSTALLED_APPS` as `'django.contrib.auth'`.

The `todos` app you built earlier stored all todos globally. By the end of this course, todos will belong to the user who created them.

First, confirm auth is wired up. In `todosite/settings.py`, `INSTALLED_APPS` should already contain `'django.contrib.auth'` and `'django.contrib.contenttypes'`. Check for them.
steps.starter_code:
INSTALLED_APPS = [
    'django.contrib.admin',
    'django.contrib.auth',
    'django.contrib.contenttypes',
    'django.contrib.sessions',
    'django.contrib.messages',
    'django.contrib.staticfiles',
    'todos',
]

DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.sqlite3',
        'NAME': BASE_DIR / 'db.sqlite3',
    }
}

SECRET_KEY = 'your-secret-key-here'

LOGIN_URL = '/login/'
LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL = '/'
steps.solution_code:
INSTALLED_APPS = [
    'django.contrib.admin',
    'django.contrib.auth',
    'django.contrib.contenttypes',
    'django.contrib.sessions',
    'django.contrib.messages',
    'django.contrib.staticfiles',
    'todos',
]

DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.sqlite3',
        'NAME': BASE_DIR / 'db.sqlite3',
    }
}

SECRET_KEY = 'your-secret-key-here'

LOGIN_URL = '/login/'
LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL = '/'
steps.hint: Add `LOGIN_URL = '/login/'` and `LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL = '/'` at the bottom of `settings.py`. `LOGIN_URL` is where unauthenticated users are sent when they hit a protected route. `LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL` is where they land after a successful login.

steps.title: Add auth URLs
steps.instruction:
## Connecting the auth URLs

Django's auth views already exist. You just need to connect them to URLs.

The built-in `django.contrib.auth.urls` module provides:
- `/login/` - renders a login form, handles POST, redirects on success
- `/logout/` - clears the session and redirects
- `/password-change/` and others (not needed for now)

You can use Django's built-in login view directly, or write your own. For this course, you will write your own login and signup views so you understand what is happening inside them.

Update `todosite/urls.py` to add routes for login, logout, and signup. You will create the view functions in the next steps.
steps.starter_code:
from django.contrib import admin
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    path('admin/', admin.site.urls),
    path('', include('todos.urls')),
]
steps.solution_code:
from django.contrib import admin
from django.urls import path, include
from todos import views as todo_views

urlpatterns = [
    path('admin/', admin.site.urls),
    path('', include('todos.urls')),
    path('login/', todo_views.login_view, name='login'),
    path('logout/', todo_views.logout_view, name='logout'),
    path('signup/', todo_views.signup_view, name='signup'),
]
steps.hint: Import `views` from your `todos` app and add three URL patterns for login, logout, and signup.

steps.title: Write the login view
steps.instruction:
## How login works

Django's session-based auth follows this pattern:

1. User visits `/login/` - you render a form
2. User submits username and password via POST
3. You call `authenticate(request, username=username, password=password)`
4. If it returns a `User` object (not `None`), call `login(request, user)` to attach the user to the session
5. Redirect to the homepage

`authenticate()` does two things: it looks up the user by username, then verifies the password against the stored hash using `check_password()`. You never handle raw passwords yourself.

If authentication fails, show an error and re-render the form.

Add the `login_view` function to `todos/views.py`.
steps.hint: After a successful `authenticate()`, call `login(request, user)` to start the session, then redirect. If authenticate returns None, set an error message.

steps.title: Write the signup view
steps.instruction:
## Signing up new users

Django provides `UserCreationForm` - a form that renders username, password, and confirm-password fields, and handles validation (passwords must match, username must be unique, etc.).

The signup flow:
1. GET request: render `UserCreationForm()` (empty form)
2. POST request: bind form with `UserCreationForm(request.POST)`
3. Call `form.is_valid()` - checks all validation rules
4. If valid: `form.save()` creates the user with the hashed password, then log them in automatically and redirect
5. If invalid: re-render the form with validation errors

Note that `UserCreationForm` calls `set_password()` internally when you call `form.save()`. The plain-text password never touches your database.

Add the `signup_view` function to `todos/views.py`.
steps.hint: After `form.save()`, immediately call `login(request, user)` to log the new user in automatically, then redirect to the index.

steps.title: Protect routes with @login_required
steps.instruction:
## Locking down your views

`@login_required` is a decorator that checks whether `request.user.is_authenticated` is `True`. If the user is anonymous, they get redirected to the URL in `LOGIN_URL` (which you set to `/login/` in settings).

Import it from `django.contrib.auth.decorators` and apply it to any view that should be private. Anonymous users will never reach the view body - Django handles the redirect automatically.

Apply `@login_required` to `index`, `add_todo`, and `delete_todo`. Leave `login_view`, `logout_view`, and `signup_view` undecorated - users need to reach those without being logged in.

You can also use `request.user` inside your views to get the currently logged-in user. In the next step you will use it to filter todos.
steps.hint: Add `@login_required` directly above the `def` line for `index`, `add_todo`, and `delete_todo`. Make sure to import it from `django.contrib.auth.decorators`.

steps.title: Per-user todos
steps.instruction:
## Owning your data

Right now, every user sees every todo. You need to add a foreign key on the `Todo` model that links each todo to the user who created it.

Django's `User` model lives in `django.contrib.auth.models`. Reference it with:

```python
from django.contrib.auth.models import User

class Todo(models.Model):
    user = models.ForeignKey(User, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    text = models.CharField(max_length=200)
```

`on_delete` controls what happens to todos if the related user is deleted. The main options are:
- `models.CASCADE` - delete the todos too (most common)
- `models.PROTECT` - block the user deletion if they have todos
- `models.SET_NULL` - set the `user` field to `null` (requires `null=True` on the column)

After updating the model:
- `request.user` in your views is the logged-in User object
- Create todos with `Todo.objects.create(text=text, user=request.user)`
- Filter the list with `Todo.objects.filter(user=request.user)`
- Only delete your own todos: `Todo.objects.filter(pk=todo_id, user=request.user).delete()`

In your templates, `{{ request.user.username }}` shows the current user's name.

Update the `Todo` model to add the user foreign key, then update the three protected views to use `request.user`.
steps.solution_code:
from django.db import models
from django.contrib.auth.models import User

class Todo(models.Model):
    user = models.ForeignKey(User, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    text = models.CharField(max_length=200)

    def __str__(self):
        return self.text
steps.hint: Import `User` from `django.contrib.auth.models` and add `user = models.ForeignKey(User, on_delete=models.CASCADE)` to your `Todo` model.

steps.title: Update views and templates
steps.hint: Change `Todo.objects.all()` to `Todo.objects.filter(user=request.user)`. Then pass `user=request.user` when creating todos, and add `user=request.user` to the delete filter.
